items.title: Obstructive pathophysiology
items.contentHtml:
Pathophysiology of appendix obstruction

  • Luminal obstruction due to fecalith (most common), lymphoid hyperplasia, foreign body or tumor.
  • Continuous mucous secretion → distension → increased intraluminal pressure → venous and lymphatic involvement → wall ischemia.
  • Bacterial proliferation (aerobes and anaerobes) → necrosis and perforation if intervention is not performed within 24-48 h.
  • Pearl examination: initial epigastric/periumbilical visceral pain (T10) moves to FID due to parietal peritoneal irritation (L1).

items.title: McBurney sign
items.contentHtml:
McBurney's sign and painful point

  • McBurney's point: middle third of the line between the navel and the right anterior superior iliac spine.
  • Positive sign: pain on deep palpation at that point, associated with peritoneal irritation.
  • Other semiological signs: Rovsing (pain in FID when compressing FII), Psoas (pain when extending the right hip), Obturator (pain when internally rotating the flexed hip).
  • Examination trap: McBurney's point may vary depending on the position of the appendix (retrocecal, pelvic); limited sensitivity.

items.title: Alvarado Scale
items.contentHtml:
Alvarado scale and diagnostic criteria

  • Criteria (total score 10): migratory pain (1), anorexia (1), nausea/vomiting (1), FID pain (2), rebound (1), fever ≥37.3°C (1), leukocytosis >10,000 (2), left deviation >75% (1).
  • Score ≤4: low probability; 5-6: probable, requires image; 7-10: high probability, surgery.
  • Pearl: in fertile women, the scale has less specificity; Modified Alvarado or RIPASA score is preferred in some centers.

items.foco: Atypical clinic in elderly and pregnant women
items.title: Atypical clinic
items.contentHtml:
Atypical symptoms in the elderly and pregnant women

  • Elderly: vague pain, absence of fever and significant leukocytosis; late presentation with perforation and abscess (high mortality).
  • Pregnant: the pregnant uterus displaces the appendix upward and laterally; pain in right upper quadrant or flank; MRI of choice if ultrasound is inconclusive (without radiation).
  • Check examination: in pregnancy, physiological leukocytosis (up to 16,000) reduces the usefulness of the count; prioritize clinical and image.

items.title: DDx: mesenteric adenitis
items.contentHtml:
Differential diagnosis with mesenteric adenitis

  • Mesenteric adenitis: more common in children and adolescents, associated with viral infection (pharyngitis, gastroenteritis).
  • Clinic: FID pain similar to appendicitis, but with low fever and adenopathies on ultrasound (nodes >5 mm in mesentery).
  • DDx key: ultrasound shows normal appendix (<6 mm) and reactive lymph nodes; absence of appendiceal inflammatory signs.
  • Pearl examination: does not require surgery; symptomatic management and observation.

items.title: CRP and leukocytosis
items.contentHtml:
CRP and leukocytosis in diagnosis

  • Leukocytosis (>10,000/mm³) with left deviation: sensitivity ~80%, specificity ~60%.
  • High CRP (>10 mg/L) has sensitivity ~75% and specificity ~70%; combination of both increases predictive value.
  • Pearl test: PCR >50 mg/L is associated with complicated appendicitis (gangrenous or perforated).
  • Limitation: normal values ​​do not rule out appendicitis (especially in the elderly or immunosuppressed).

items.title: US vs CT in appendicitis
items.contentHtml:
Ultrasound vs computed tomography

  • Ultrasound: first line in children and pregnant women; findings: appendix >6 mm, non-compressible, wall thickening, free fluid. Sensitivity ~85% (operator-dependent).
  • CT with contrast: gold standard, sensitivity >95%; key finding: distended appendix >7 mm with wall enhancement and fatty strangulation.
  • Trap examination: routine CT not indicated if typical clinical features; Reserve for atypical cases or suspected complications.

items.title: Simple vs complicated classification
items.contentHtml:
Classification: simple vs complicated appendicitis

  • Simple: inflammation without necrosis or perforation; management with early appendectomy or antibiotic therapy (in selected uncomplicated cases).
  • Complicated: gangrenous, perforated or abscess; requires urgent surgery and broad-spectrum antibiotics (e.g. ceftriaxone + metronidazole).
  • Pearl examination: in plastron (inflammatory mass), initial conservative management with antibiotics and delayed appendectomy after 6-8 weeks.

items.foco: Surgical treatment: open vs laparoscopic appendectomy
items.title: Open vs laparoscopic
items.contentHtml:
Surgical treatment: open vs laparoscopic appendectomy

  • Laparoscopic: preferred (less postoperative pain, shorter stay, less wound infection); contraindicated in uncorrected coagulopathy or severe generalized peritonitis.
  • Open: indicated if large plastron, retrocecal appendix, or in patients with previous extensive abdominal surgery.
  • Pearl examination: in complicated appendicitis, laparoscopy reduces intra-abdominal abscesses if performed with adequate irrigation.

items.title: Complication: appendiceal abscess
items.contentHtml:
Complications: appendiceal abscess

  • Abscess: localized purulent collection; management with percutaneous drainage guided by CT or US + antibiotics (piperacillin/tazobactam or carbapenem).
  • If drainage not feasible or fails, surgery (appendectomy + drainage).
  • Pearl examination: abscess culture typically polymicrobial (E. coli, Bacteroides fragilis); cover anaerobes.

items.title: Peritonitis and plastron
items.contentHtml:
Peritonitis and appendicular plastron

  • Generalized peritonitis: due to perforation; requires urgent laparotomy or laparoscopy, peritoneal lavage and intravenous antibiotics (ceftriaxone + metronidazole or ampicillin/sulbactam).
  • Appendicular plastron: inflammatory mass of the appendix, omentum and loops; conservative management with antibiotics (e.g. ertapenem) and fasting; Delayed appendectomy in 6-8 weeks.
  • Examination trap: do not operate on plastron in the acute phase (risk of intestinal injury and dissemination).
